coredata.dc:title: A Carbon-Dot Sensing Probe for Screening of Date Rape Drugs: Nitro-containing Benzodiazepines
coredata.dc:creator: Yen, Yao-Te
Lin, Yu-Syuan
Chen, Tzu-Heng
Chyueh, San-Chong
Chang, Huan-Tsung
coredata.dc:description: Simple, low-cost, rapid, selective, and sensitive sensing kits are required for screening of illicit date rape drugs such as nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, clonazepam, and nitrazepam in beverages. Because of low solubility of these analytes in aqueous solution, hydrophobic carbon dots (hC-dots) are needed. In this study, hC-dots are prepared from D-phenylalanine through a hydrothermal route, which have been used for screening of the analytes. To minimize matrix interference in various beverages, including beer, red wine, whisky, and orange juice, liquid-liquid extraction of the analytes using toluene for 5 min is required before quantitation based on analyte induced fluorescence quenching of the hC-dots at 430 nm when excited at 365 nm. This assay allows the quantitation of nimetazepam down to 7.24 μM, which is lower than that found in the beverages associated with drug-facilitated sexual assault (DFSA). The assay is selective toward nitro-substituted benzodiazepines over popular abused drugs, including 4-chloroethcathinone, cocaine, heroin, 4-hydroxybutyric acid, ketamine, and methamphetamine. Since only hC-dots (dispersed in 0.5 mL toluene), a handheld UV light (365 nm), and a camera (or smartphone) are required, this simple, low-cost, rapid, and selective sensing system is ideal for beverage analyses at DFSA crime scenes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: date rape drugs
carbon dots
sensor
nimetazepam
benzodiazepines

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of nimetazepam in standard solution

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful to the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan and the Ministry of Justice (MOJ) of Taiwan for providing financial support for this study under the MOST contracts 107-2113-M-002-015-MY3 and 108-2622-M-002-002 -CC2 ; under the MOJ contracts 108-1301-05-17-05 . The assistance of Ms. Ya-Yun Yang and Ms. Ching-Yen Lin from the Instrument Center of National Taiwan University (NTU) for TEM measurement is appreciated. Appendix A

We are grateful to the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan and the Ministry of Justice (MOJ) of Taiwan for providing financial support for this study under the MOST contracts 107-2113-M-002-015-MY3 and 108-2622-M-002-002 -CC2; under the MOJ contracts 108-1301-05-17-05. The assistance of Ms. Ya-Yun Yang and Ms. Ching-Yen Lin from the Instrument Center of National Taiwan University (NTU) for TEM measurement is appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A rapid and simple hC-dot sensing system in conjunction with LLE for quantitation of nimetazepam.

Characteristics of hC-dots. (A) TEM image, (B) XPS spectrum, (C) FT-IR spectrum, and (D) Raman spectrum. Inset (a) in (A) is the size distribution of hC-dots (130 counts). Inset (b) in (A) is the HRTEM of hC-dots.

Detection of nimetazepam using the hC-dots. (A) PL emission spectra of the hC-dots in toluene in the (a) absence and (b) presence of 100 μM nimetazepam at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm; (B) sensitivity for nimetazepam. (C) Selectivity of the hC-dots for nimetazepam over the tested solutes, in which the concentrations of alprazolam, nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, clonazepam and nitrazepam are 100 μM, and that for others are 1 mM. The inset in (B) is the relationship of (F0 − F)/F0 of the hC-dots against the nimetazepam concentration, which is linear over the range 33.9–296.3 μM; F and F0 are the PL intensities at 430 nm of the hC-dots in the presence and absence of nimetazepam, respectively. The error bars represent standard deviations for three repeated experiments.

(A) A Stern–Volmer plot (left) and a dynamic Stern–Volmer plot (right) of the hC-dots in the presence of nimetazepam. (B) Absorption spectra of (a) nimetazepam, (b) flunitrazepam, (c) alprazolam, and (d) PL spectra of the hC-dots excited at 365 nm. Spectra represented as blue dots are predicted using prolonging original spectra. Cyclic voltammograms of (C) hC-dots (66 μg mL−1), and (D) 1 mM nimetazepam, flunitrazepam and alprazolam. (E) HOMO and LUMO electron distribution of nimetazepam using the DFT calculation.

(A) (F0 − F)/F0 of the hC-dots against nimetazepam in the concentration range of 33.9–296.3 μM in beer, red wine, orange juice, and whiskey, separately, in which F and F0 are the PL intensities at 430 nm of the hC-dots in the presence and absence of nimetazepam, respectively. (B) PL images of the hC-dots in various beverages with/without containing (a) 85 μM and (b) 170 μM of nimetazepam. The aqueous and organic phases in each vial are on the bottom (no PL) and top, respectively. The samples without and with containing the drug are on the left and right sides, respectively, in each image. (C) Images of ICA analysis of nimetazepam (85 μM) spiked in beer, orange juice, whiskey, and red wine, separately.
HOMOs, LUMOs, and Band Gaps of hC-dots and Analytes.
The inter- and intra-day assays for the analysis of nimetazepam in beer, red wine, orange juice, and whiskey (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aIntra-day assay was performed by measuring the concentration of spiked samples on the same day.
bInter-day assays were performed by measuring the concentration of spiked samples on day 1, 3, and 5.
cAccuracy was assessed by determining the recovery percentage (determined value/added value × 100%) of spiked samples.
dThe precision of the inter-day and intra-day assays were assessed by relative standard deviation (RSD).

head.ce:title: A Carbon-Dot Sensing Probe for Screening of Date Rape Drugs: Nitro-containing Benzodiazepines

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10617, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, Taipei 10617, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Forensic Science, Investigation Bureau, Ministry of Justice, Xindian Dist, New Taipei City 23149, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Forensic Science, Investigation Bureau, Ministry of Justice, Xindian Dist, New Taipei City 23149, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Chung Yuan Christian University, Chungli District, Taoyuan City 32023, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Chung Yuan Christian University, Chungli District, Taoyuan City 32023, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, 1, Section 4, Roosevelt Road, Taipei 10617, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simple, low-cost, rapid, selective, and sensitive sensing kits are required for screening of illicit date rape drugs such as nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, clonazepam, and nitrazepam in beverages. Because of low solubility of these analytes in aqueous solution, hydrophobic carbon dots (hC-dots) are needed. In this study, hC-dots are prepared from D-phenylalanine through a hydrothermal route, which have been used for screening of the analytes. To minimize matrix interference in various beverages, including beer, red wine, whisky, and orange juice, liquid-liquid extraction of the analytes using toluene for 5 min is required before quantitation based on analyte induced fluorescence quenching of the hC-dots at 430 nm when excited at 365 nm. This assay allows the quantitation of nimetazepam down to 7.24 μM, which is lower than that found in the beverages associated with drug-facilitated sexual assault (DFSA). The assay is selective toward nitro-substituted benzodiazepines over popular abused drugs, including 4-chloroethcathinone, cocaine, heroin, 4-hydroxybutyric acid, ketamine, and methamphetamine. Since only hC-dots (dispersed in 0.5 mL toluene), a handheld UV light (365 nm), and a camera (or smartphone) are required, this simple, low-cost, rapid, and selective sensing system is ideal for beverage analyses at DFSA crime scenes.

ce:section.ce:para: Illicit drugs such as benzodiazepines have caused serious social problems in many countries [1]. For example, alprazolam, clonazepam, flunitrazepam, nimetazepam, and nitrazepam have been used for drug-facilitated sexual assault (DFSA), although they are anxiolytic (hypnotic) drugs for treating sleep disorders [2–5]. These drugs are usually spiked into beverages to incapacitate victims. Detection of the spiked drugs is difficult, mainly because of their low concentrations and complicated sample matrices. Immunoassay kits are commonly used for the detection of abused drugs, but they are expensive, and their sensitivity and specificity may suffer from serious matrix interference [6]. Electrochemical approaches have been used for the detection of DFSA-related drugs with high sensitivity, but matrix interference and electrode fouling are concerns [7–9]. Although gas chromatography mass spectrometry (GC–MS) and liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (MS–LC) have been used for the detection of drugs in spiked beverages, these methods are expensive and inconvenient for on-site analysis [10–13]. Thus, developing low-cost and simple sensing systems for screening low concentrations of benzodiazepines in various beverages is required.

Colorimetric assays using nanoparticles have been developed for the detection of illicit drugs based on analyte-induced aggregation of the nanoparticles or disassembly of the aggregates [14–16]. Alternatively, photoluminescent nanomaterials, including NaYF4:Yb,Er upconversion nanoparticles, Ag nanoclusters, as well as CdSe and ZnS quantum dots have been employed for the sensing of illicit drugs such as codeine, cocaine, and ketamine [17–20]. To provide selectivity, conjugation of these nanomaterials with expensive recognition elements such as aptamers is usually required. In addition, matrix interference is a common problem when applying these nanomaterials for sensing drugs in biological samples.

Low-cost carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted interest because of their ease in preparation, low cost, photoluminescence (PL), biocompatibility, and resistance to salt- and photoirradiation-induced quenching [21–24]. C-dots that are usually formed through oxidation, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation processes have sphere-like shapes with average sizes of 1–10 nm [25,26]. They possess rich functional groups on their surfaces, depending on the precursor, synthetic method, and experimental condition [27,28]. Functional groups can be used for conjugation with recognition elements to improve their selectivity toward analytes of interest. C-dots have been used in sensing of various analytes and cell imaging [29–32]. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report about their use for the quantitation of illicit drugs, especially in complicated samples. C-dots prepared from various hydrophilic precursors through hydrothermal and electrochemical routes are usually dispersed in aqueous solutions [33–37]. Although they have high fluorescence quantum yields (>30%) [38], their use for the quantitation of hydrophobic illicit drugs such as benzodiazepines in beverages is problematic because of their weak interactions with these drugs. Thus, preparation of hydrophobic C-dots (hC-dots) with strong fluorescence is required for sensing of hydrophobic illicit drugs.

In this study, we developed a turn-off PL sensing assay using hC-dots in conjunction with liquid-liquid extraction (LLE) for the screening of nitro-containing benzodiazepines. The hC-dots were prepared from D-phenylalanine through a hydrothermal approach. To provide high sensitivity and selectivity, important experimental parameters, including polarity, extractant volume, and amounts of hC-dots, were optimized. To gain more information about the sensing mechanism, UV-vis absorption, PL, and cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements of the hC-dots and analytes were conducted. This simple sensing system was then validated for the quantitation of nimetazepam in spiked beverages, showing its potential for on-site screening of nitro-containing benzodiazepines at crime scenes.

D-phenylalanine, ferrocene, quinine sulfate monohydrate, and tetrabutyl ammonium hexafluorophosphate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). Beer (Taiwan Beer), red wine (GatoNegro), and whiskey (Taiwan Tobacco and Liquor Corporation), with alcohol contents (v/v) of 5%, 13.5%, and 40%, respectively, were purchased from a local supermarket. Orange juice (Minute Maid) was purchased from a local market. Alprazolam, clonazepam, flunitrazepam, 4-hydroxybutyric acid (GHB) sodium salt, nimetazepam, and nitrazepam, all at the concentration of 1 mg mL−1 in methanol, were purchased from Cerilliant (Round Rock, TX, USA). Dimethyl sulfoxide, ethanol, and toluene were purchased from J.T. Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). Ultrapure water (18.2 mΩ·cm) from an ultrapure water system (Milli-Q, Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) was used to prepare the aqueous solutions in this study. High purities of 4-chloroethcathinone, cocaine, heroin, ketamine, and methamphetamine (MA) were all in hydrochloride salt forms and were provided by the Investigation Bureau, Ministry of Justice, Taiwan. An immunochromatographic assay (ICA) kit for the detection of benzodiazepines was purchased from Formosa Biomedical Technology Corporation (Taipei, Taiwan).

Through a hydrothermal route, hC-dots were synthesized from D-phenylalanine. Briefly, 1 g of D-phenylalanine was added into a Teﬂon-lined stainless-steel autoclave containing 35 mL of toluene, which was then subjected to heating at 240 °C for 14 h in an oven. After being cooled to ambient temperature (25 °C), the obtained brownish-yellow solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm polytetrafluoroethylene membrane to remove large particles. The solution was then purified using a mixture of ethanol and ultrapure water (v/v: 1:1) to extract and eliminate unreacted reactant and byproducts several times until no fluorescence in the extractant was detected using a portable UV-lamp (BD405, BD-TECK, Taichung City, Taiwan) [39,40]. The resulting solution was dried by rotary evaporation, and the resulting yellow powder was dispersed in 20 mL of toluene to obtain a purified hC-dot solution. After the solution (1 mL) was evaporated, the hC-dot powder was weighed to determine its concentration as 1.58 mg mL−1. Aliquots (5 mL) of the as-purified hC-dots were dried and redispersed separately in 5 mL of ethyl acetate and dichloromethane, which were then stored at 4 °C in the dark prior to use. Fluorescence quantum yields (Φf) of the hC-dots dispersed separately in toluene, ethyl acetate, and dichloromethane at the excitation/emission wavelengths of 365/430 nm were determined by comparing them with quinine sulfate (Φf = 0.54) dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4.

A spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse) from Varian (Palo Alto, CA, USA) was used to record the emission and excitation spectra of the hC-dot solutions under various conditions. An ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectrophotometer (Evolution 220) from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA) was used to record the absorption spectra of the hC-dot solutions. A Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FT-IR) from Thermo (Nicolet iS5, MA, USA) was employed to determine the functional groups present on the surface of the hC-dots. A Raman system from Thermo (Waltham, MA, USA) consisting of a microscope (Olympus BX51) equipped with a 40× objective (numerical aperture 0.6) and a light source at a wavelength of 780 nm was employed to obtain the Raman spectra of the hC-dots deposited on the wafers. The Raman spectra were obtained from an average of three accumulations, each with an integration time of 16 s. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the hC-dots were obtained using an FEI Tecnai-G2-F20 (GCE Market, Blackwood, NJ, USA) operated at 200 kV. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the hC-dots were recorded using an FEI Talos F200× from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, USA). Before performing TEM measurements, aliquots (10 μL) of the purified hC-dot solution were deposited on 400-mesh carbon-coated Cu grids, and then excess solvent was evaporated at ambient temperature and pressure. A spectrometer (Theta Probe) from VG Scientiﬁc (East Grinstead, UK) with Al Kα X-ray radiation was used to record the X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) of the hC-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nimetazepam in standard solution
ce:section.ce:para: Mixtures (500 μL) of the as-purified hC-dot solution (50, 20 or 10 μL) and toluene (450, 480 or 490 μL) in microcentrifuge tubes (2 mL) were separately used to prepare hC-dot solutions (158, 63, or 32 μg mL−1). To prepare standard sample solutions, certain volumes of nimetazepam (1 mg mL−1) were added separately to mixtures of ethanol and ultrapure water (v/v: 7:3), with final concentrations ranging from 33.9 to 296.3 μM. To each of the hC-dot solutions (0.5 mL) in the microcentrifuge tube, aliquots (1.5 mL) of the standard sample solutions were slowly added. After being shaken by hand for 30 s and kept for 5 min, each (400 μL) of the upper toluene layer (sensing layer) of the hC-dot mixtures was transferred to a cuvette before PL measurement.

Selectivity of the hC-dot assay was assessed similar to that in the analysis of the standard solutions, but mixtures of ethanol and ultrapure water containing different abused drugs, including alprazolam, clonazepam, flunitrazepam, nitrazepam, 4-chloroethcathinone, cocaine, GHB, heroin, ketamine, and MA, were used. All experiments were performed under ambient conditions.

The Stern–Volmer equation (eq 1) was used to assess the fluorescence quenching behavior as follows [41]:
(1)
F 0
/ F
=1+
K sv
[Q]
where F0 and F are the PL intensities of hC-dots in the absence and presence of nimetazepam, respectively, [Q] is the concentration of nimetazepam, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant. A time-resolved absorption and emission spectroscope with a photon-counting system and a laser (375 nm) from Time-Harp 200 (PicoQuant GmbH, Berlin, Germany) was used to measure the florescence lifetimes at the emission wavelength of 430 nm of hC-dot solutions without and with containing nimetazepam in a concentration range of 33.9–296.3 μM. The dynamic quenching of hC-dots induced by nimetazepam was evaluated using the following equation (eq 2) [41,42]:
(2)
τ 0
/ τ
=1+
K D
Q
=1+
k q
τ 0
Q
where
τ 0
and τ are the average lifetimes of hC-dots in the absence and presence of nimetazepam, respectively, KD is the dynamic Stern-Volmer constant, and kq is the quenching rate constant.

The absorption spectra of hC-dots (32 μg mL−1) in the absence and presence of nimetazepam (296.3 μM) were recorded to investigate the interaction of nimetazepam and hC-dots. In addition, the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of hC-dots, nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, and alprazolam were estimated. A mixture (10 mL) containing tetrabutyl ammonium hexafluorophosphate (0.1 M) and dimethyl sulfoxide was used to separately prepare hC-dots (0.79 mg mL−1), nimetazepam (1 mM), flunitrazepam (1 mM), alprazolam (1 mM), and ferrocene (1 mM) solutions. Cyclic voltammograms (CV) of the hC-dots, nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, alprazolam, and ferrocene were recorded using a CHI 760D electrochemical workstation from CH instruments (Austin, TX, USA) with a sweeping rate of 0.05 V s−1. The three-electrode configuration consisted of a glassy carbon electrode as a working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (saturated by KCl) electrode as a reference electrode, and a platinum wire as a counter electrode. The energy gaps of the hC-dots, nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, and alprazolam were estimated separately by recording the absorption spectra of the hC-dots (0.32 mg mL−1 in toluene), nimetazepam (10−4 M in methanol), flunitrazepam (10−4 M in methanol), and alprazolam (10−4 M in methanol).

To prepare the nimetazepam-spiked samples, aliquots (20–175 μL) of nimetazepam (1 mg mL−1) were added separately to 1980–1825 μL of beer, red wine, orange juice, or whiskey, with final nimetazepam concentrations ranging from 33.9 to 296.3 μM. Real samples (1949.8 and 1899.6 μL) spiked separately with 50.2 and 100.4 μL of 1 mg mL−1 nimetazepam (final concentrations of 85 and 170 μM) were used for the intra- and inter-day analyses to determine the recovery percentages (determined value/added value × 100%) and relative standard deviations (RSD) of this approach. The intra-day analysis was conducted through three measurements, and the inter-day analysis was conducted on day 1, 3, and 5, each with three measurements. Aliquots (1.5 mL) of the spiked beverages were added separately to the hC-dot solution (0.5 mL), which were then shaken for 30 s and kept for 5 min. To record the PL images of the hC-dots after extraction of the samples with/without containing nimetazepam (85 or 170 μM) using toluene, a camera (COOLPIX A900, Nikon, Tokyo, Japan) and a portable UV lamp at 365 nm were used. Similarly, the probe was applied for screening of beverages without and with flunitrazepam (32 or 64 μM). To ensure the chemical structures of the analytes extracted using toluene (upper layer), aliquots (80 μL) of the extracts were subjected to GC–MS measurement (7890A/5975C MSD from Agilent; Santa Clara, CA, USA). For comparison, an ICA kit was used to detect nimetazepam (85 μM)-spiked samples, including beer, red wine, orange juice, and whiskey, separately. Three drops of each of the spiked samples were added onto the test spot of each ICA, which was equilibrated for 5 min prior to analysis.

The geometry, electronic properties, and energy gap of nimetazepam were obtained using Gaussian 09 [43]. A density functional theory (DFT) with the B3LYP/6-13G basis set was used for calculation. Molecular orbitals were visualized using GaussView.

The hC-dots prepared from D-phenylalanine were dispersed slightly in water but were well dispersed and stable in ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, and toluene. The spherical hC-dots, with an average size of 5.6 ± 0.8 nm (from 130 counts), were uniform, as displayed in Fig. 1 A. The HRTEM of the hC-dots displayed in inset (b) shows a lattice spacing of 0.32 nm, which corresponds to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, indicating the formation of crystallized hC-dots [44]. Fig. 1B displays the XPS spectrum of the hC-dots, with a graphitic C 1s peak at 284.6 eV and an O 1s peak at 532 eV. The deconvoluted XPS spectra of C 1s displayed in Figure S1A show four peaks attributed to the presence of carbonyl, hydroxyl, ester, and carboxylate groups on the hC-dots. The deconvoluted XPS spectra of O 1s, as shown in Figure S1B, reveals two peaks that are attributed to the carbonyl and ester/hydroxyl groups on the hC-dots. The FT-IR spectrum of the hC-dots displayed in Fig. 1C exhibits a C = O vibrational stretching band at 1654 cm−1, a C–H stretching band at 2923 cm−1, and a weak O–H vibrational stretching band between 3110 and 3430 cm−1. When compared to hydrophilic C-dots [31], the hC-dots have a weaker absorption band of O–H vibrational stretching. The Raman spectrum of the hC-dots displayed in Fig. 1D shows a D-band at 1351 cm−1 for the edge defects of carbon and a G-band at 1585 cm−1 for the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon, with a D to G band intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.8 [30,34]. Figure S2A displays the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the hC-dots in toluene, ethyl acetate, and dichloromethane. The peak at approximately 285 nm of the hC-dots in toluene corresponds to the π−π* transition [45]. Similar to most hydrophilic C-dots, Figure S2B-D displays the excitation-wavelength-dependence emission properties of the hC-dots in the three solutions when excited at wavelengths ranging from 340–420 nm [33–35]. These emission bands are mainly related to the transitions of the nonbonding orbitals of the shell in the hC-dot into their π* orbitals. The hC-dots in the three solutions have different emission intensities and emission wavelengths, indicating that solvent polarity may play a role in their optical properties. However, when separately excited at 300 and 320 nm, the PL bands both occur at 360 nm, mainly because the transition is involved in the core of the hC-dot. Like most C-dots, the emission intensities in the three solvents decrease upon increasing the excitation wavelength. Their quantum yields at 430 nm when excited at 365 nm are 16.2% in toluene, 9.8% in ethyl acetate, and 9.4% in dichloromethane. The hC-dots in the three solvents remain stable during photoirradiation. For example, after irradiation of the hC-dots in toluene using a UV lamp (365 nm, 3 W) for 2 h, the PL intensity remained almost constant (<10% variation). Based on the fluorescence quantum yield and dispersibility of the hC-dots, the extraction efficiency (discussed later) of nimetazepam from a mixture of ethanol and ultrapure water (v/v: 7:3), and safety, toluene was selected as a solvent in this study.

To optimize the sensitivity of the assay for the detection of nimetazepam (33.9 μM), hC-dots at different concentrations (32, 63, and 158 μg mL-1) in toluene were tested. The optimal concentration of hC-dots was determined to be 32 μg mL-1. At higher concentrations, the sensitivity is lower, mainly because of a high fluorescence background. On the other hand, low signal-to-noise ratios due to unstable fluorescence signals are problematic at lower concentrations.

Fig. 2 A shows the emission spectra of the hC-dots in toluene in the absence and presence of 100 μM nimetazepam when excited at 365 nm. The concentrations of hC-dots from three different batches were determined to be 32, 29, and 30 μg mL−1. Nimetazepam (100 μM) separately induced 25.4%, 23.8%, and 24.3% PL quenching of the three hC-dot solutions, with a standard deviation of 0.8%. The result shows a good batch-to-batch reproducibility of the assay. Fig. 2B shows that the PL at 430 nm, when excited at 365 nm, decreases upon increasing the concentrations of nimetazepam. The relationship of (F0 − F)/F0 of the hC-dots (R2 = 0.99) against the nimetazepam concentration is linear over the range of 33.9–296.3 μM, in which F and F0 are the PL intensities at 430 nm of the hC-dots in the presence and absence of nimetazepam, respectively. Based on 3δ/slope, in which δ represents the deviation of (F0 − F)/F0 for a blank test and the slope was obtained from the calibration curve, a limit of detection (LOD) of 7.24 μM (2.14 μg mL−1) for nimetazepam was calculated. The LOD provided by this assay for the analyte is lower than that (50 μg mL−1) usually added into beverages for DFSA.

To show the selectivity of this rapid and simple assay for the detection of nitro-containing benzodiazepines (structures shown in Figure S3), including clonazepam, flunitrazepam, and nitrazepam, popular illicit drugs, including alprazolam, 4-chloroethcathinone, cocaine, GHB, heroin, MA, and ketamine were tested as controls. Fig. 2C reveals that the nitro-containing benzodiazepines (100 μM) quenched at least 20% of the PL intensity at 430 nm of hC-dots when excited at 365 nm. Alprazolam (100 μM), ketamine (1 mM), and GHB (1 mM) did not induce PL quenching. Psychostimulants and depressants (1 mM) such as 4-chloroethcathinone, cocaine, MA, and heroin that are rarely used in DFSA did not induce PL quenching of the hC-dots. In addition, these compounds are hydrophilic [46] and thus were not extracted by toluene. Since LLE using toluene was applied to extract the hydrophobic analytes, hydrophilic additives that may exist in the tablets of benzodiazepines do not cause significant interference.

Fig. 3 A displays a Stern–Volmer and a dynamic Stern–Volmer plot of the hC-dots in the presence of nimetazepam. The slope (KSV) for the Stern–Volmer plot was determined to be 4.2 × 103 M−1 (R2 = 0.99) and the slope (KD) for the dynamic Stern–Volmer plot was determined to be 4.0 × 102 M−1 (R2 = 0.98) [47,48]. The kq value at
τ 0
of 5.3 ns was calculated to be 7.5 × 1010 M−1 S−1, which is higher than that (approximately 1.0 × 1010 M−1 S−1) of diffusion-controlled quenching, indicating the interaction of hC-dots with nimetazepam [41]. The absorption spectra of hC-dots in the absence and presence of nimetazepam (296.3 μM) are similar as shown in Figure S4, indicating that the formation of a ground-state complex between nimetazepam and hC-dots is negligible. Because the emission spectrum of the hC-dots when excited at 365 nm has little overlap with the absorption spectrum of nimetazepam or flunitrazepam, but no overlap with that of alprazolam, as shown in Fig. 3B. Thus, fluorescence energy transfer (FET) was ruled out for the quenching mechanism (Table 1 ).

To investigate whether an electron transfer occurs between nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, or alprazolam and the hC-dots when excited at 365 nm, HOMO and LUMO levels of the hC-dots, nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, and alprazolam were estimated from their corresponding CVs, with results displayed in Fig. 3C and 3D [49]. The oxidation potentials of the ferrocene, hC-dots, nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, and alprazolam were determined to be 0.53, 0.24, 1.22, 1.27, and 1.16 eV, respectively [50,51]. Their HOMOs were calculated using the equation EHOMO = − (4.8 − EOX,Fc/Fc + + EOX), where EOX,Fc/Fc + is the oxidation potential of ferrocene/ferrocene+ (shown in Figure S5) and EOX is the oxidation potential of the tested chemicals. For example, the HOMO of hC-dots was calculated to be -4.51 eV. Their LUMOs were calculated using the equation ELUMO = EHOMO + E0–0onset /Eg, separately, where E0–0onset represents the 0–0 energy that is defined as the lowest energy transition estimated from the longest absorption wavelength (λonset) of nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, or alprazolam [51–53]. Figure S6 displays the Tauc plot of hC-dots, which was used to calculate the Eg to be 2.90 eV [54–57]. Thus, the LUMO of hC-dots was calculated to be -1.61 eV.

In addition, DFT calculation of the electronic states of nimetazepam was performed to acquire the electron distribution of its HOMO and LUMO [43]. As shown in Fig. 3E, the HOMO orbitals are mainly located on the fusion of a benzene ring and a diazepine ring. Because nimetazepam and flunitrazepam both have fusion moieties, their HOMO levels are close. The LUMO orbitals are located equally on the structures of benzodiazepines, leading to similar LUMO levels of nimetazepam and flunitrazepam. Table 2 summarizes the values of the HOMOs and LUMOs and the energy gaps of the hC-dots, nimetazepam, flunitrazepam, and alprazolam. The energy gap (3.44 eV) obtained from the absorption spectrum of nimetazepam is close to that (3.28 eV) calculated from the DFT calculation. The HOMO of flunitrazepam is slightly lower than that of nimetazepam, mainly because fluorine exists in its phenyl group. Because the LUMO levels of nimetazepam and flunitrazepam are close and both lower than that of the hC-dots, an electron transfer from the hC-dots to the two analysts likely occurs after excited at 365 nm [49,57]. On the other hand, the LUMO level of alprazolam is higher than that of the hC-dots, revealing that an electron transfer from the hC-dots to alprazolam did not occur.

Because of the complicated matrix and colors of various beverages, extraction of nimetazepam is required prior to its fluorescence measurement. Toluene, ethyl acetate, and dichloromethane were separately used to perform LLE of nimetazepam in beer, red wine, orange juice, and whiskey. Ethyl acetate is miscible with the high alcohol contents of beverages such as whiskey or red wine. Although dichloromethane (0.5 mL) provides a higher extraction efficiency than toluene (0.5 mL) for a 1.5 mL standard solution containing 100 μM nimetazepam (99.4% vs. 94.2%), it was not selected in this study because of its toxicity. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield of hC-dots is smaller in dichloromethane (9.4%) than in toluene (16.2%). With respect to the extraction efficiency, amount of toluene used, and sensitivity, we found that 0.5 mL of toluene is optimal for the extraction of nimetazepam from a 1.5 mL aqueous solution. The rapid and simple hC-dot sensing system as shown in Scheme 1 , with the advantages of robust, sensitivity and selectivity, was applied to the detection of nimetazepam added into various beverages, including beer, red wine, whiskey, and orange juice.

Fig. 4 A displays the relationships of (F0 − F)/F0 of the hC-dots against the nimetazepam over a range of 33.9–296.3 μM in beer, red wine, orange juice, and whiskey, separately, in which F and F0 are the PL intensities at 430 nm of the hC-dots in the presence and absence of nimetazepam, respectively. The slopes of their calibration curves and LODs are 2.1 × 10−3 (R2 = 0.99) and 15.4 μM, respectively, in beer, 2.1 × 10−3 (R2 = 0.99) and 10.6 μM, respectively, in red wine, 2.1 × 10−3 (R2 = 0.99) and 10.9 μM, respectively, in orange juice, and 2.1 × 10−3 (R2 = 0.99) and 12.6 μM, respectively, in whiskey, where the LODs are represented based on 3δ/slope; δ represents the deviation of (F0 − F)/F0 for a blank test, and the slope was obtained separately from each of the calibration curves for the spiked beverage samples. The obtained slopes and LODs are separately close to that (2.0 × 10−3 and 7.24 μM) obtained from the standard solution, showing insignificant matrix interference of the assay.

Table 2 summarizes the mean values, accuracy, and precision of intra- and inter-day assays using the hC-dots for the analysis of nimetazepam in the tested beverages (n = 3). The mean concentrations of 85 μM [one tablet (5 mg) of nimetazepam was dissolved in a 200 mL beverage] and 170 μM of the tested real samples were determined separately to be in the ranges of 83.3–90.9 and 175.8–185.5 μM for the intra-day assay, with precision values of 4.1–7.5% and 2.6–5.0%. For the inter-day assay, the mean concentrations were determined separately to be in the ranges of 82.6–88.6 and 180.0–183.9 μM, with precision values of 2.2–6.8% and 1.3–1.9%. The RSD values obtained for both intra- and inter-day precisions are <10%. Fig. 4B shows the PL images of the hC-dots in the real samples with/without nimetazepam, which were taken with a camera under excitation with a UV lamp (365 nm). Samples with or without containing nimetazepam (85 μM or 170 μM) were easily distinguished by the naked eye. The nimetazepam extracted from various samples (spiked with 85 μM nimetazepam) induced various PL quenching efficiencies: 23.2 (±1.1)% in beer, 22.5 (±1.1)% in red wine, 23.3 (±1.0)% in orange juice, and 23.5 (±1.4)% in whiskey. The extracted nimetazepam from the samples spiked with 170 μM nimetazepam induced higher PL quenching efficiencies of the hC-dots: 43.7 (±1.8)% in beer, 42.5 (±1.1)% in red wine, 42.8 (±1.1)% in orange juice, and 43.3 (±1.0)% in whiskey. The results reveal the concentration dependence of PL quenching and negligible matrix interference of the assay. In addition, the assay was further validated by the analysis of beverages containing flunitrazepam [32 and 64 μM; one (2 mg) and two tablets (4 mg) of flunitrazepam were dissolved separately in 200-mL beverage samples], as shown in Figure S7. We obtained different PL quenching efficiencies of hC-dots induced by the extracted flunitrazepam at the two different concentrations from various beverages: 8.4 (±2.2)% and 16.2 (±1.4)% in beer, 8.2 (±1.6)% and 15.9 (±0.7)% in red wine, 7.9 (±0.4)% and 16.3 (±1.6) % in orange juice, and 8.1 (±1.0)% and 16.9 (±2.1)% in whiskey. We note that it is easy to visually differentiate the samples with and without flunitrazepam at concentrations higher than 64 μM.

To ensure the extraction and sensing results, the extracted analytes were subjected to GC–MS measurements. Figure S8 shows a representative GC–MS chromatogram and MS spectrum for nimetazepam extracted from whiskey. The results confirmed that the analyte was extracted by toluene. Because the concentrations of nimetazepam in beverages for DFSA are higher than 170 μM, our results reveal the potential of this simple and rapid assay for DFSA analysis. The total analysis time for one sample is less than 5 min. Fig. 4C displays false-negative results when using the ICA kit for the detection of nimetazepam (85 μM) spiked separately in whiskey and red wine. When compared to the analysis using the ICA kit, our probe shows negligible matrix interference. In addition, our probe offers the advantages of low cost, rapidity, easy storage, and quality assurance. Overall, our results reveal that the rapid and simple sensing system consisting of only hC-dots dispersed in toluene and a handheld UV light source has potential for screening of nitro-containing benzodiazepines at DFSA crime scenes.

Low-cost hC-dots prepared from D-phenylalanine are sensitive for the quantitation of nimetazepam after it is extracted from various beverages with toluene. Based on analyte induced PL quenching, the hC-dots allow detection of nimetazepam down to 7.24 μM, which is much lower than that found in most DFSA crime scenes. In addition to sensitivity, the assay is selective toward nitro-substituted benzodiazepines. Because only hC-dots dispersed in toluene and a handheld UV light source are needed for the analysis, this simple, low-cost, rapid, and selective assay is ideal for DFSA analysis.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.
The Transparency document associated with this article can be found in the online version.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST) of Taiwan and the Ministry of Justice (MOJ) of Taiwan for providing financial support for this study under the MOST contracts 107-2113-M-002-015-MY3 and 108-2622-M-002-002 -CC2; under the MOJ contracts 108-1301-05-17-05. The assistance of Ms. Ya-Yun Yang and Ms. Ching-Yen Lin from the Instrument Center of National Taiwan University (NTU) for TEM measurement is appreciated.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvothermal synthesis of phosphorus and nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for iron (III)
Microchim Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.M. Omer, D.I. Tofiq, A.Q. Hassan, Solvothermal synthesis of phosphorus and nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for iron (III), Microchim Acta 185 (2018) 466.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple method of measuring the band gap energy value of TiO2 in the powder form using a UV/Vis/NIR spectrometer, Application Note Shelton

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Dharma, A. Pisal, C. Shelton, Simple method of measuring the band gap energy value of TiO2 in the powder form using a UV/Vis/NIR spectrometer, Application Note Shelton, CT: PerkinElmer (2009).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots as new hole transport material for perovskite solar cells
Synth Met.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Paulo, G. Stoica, W. Cambarau, E. Martinez-Ferrero, E. Palomares, Carbon quantum dots as new hole transport material for perovskite solar cells, Synth Met. 222 (2016) 17-22.

PCCP

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Zeng, W. Zhang, D.M. Arvapalli, B. Bloom, A. Sheardy, T. Mabe, et al., A fluorescence-electrochemical study of carbon nanodots (CNDs) in bio-and photoelectronic applications and energy gap investigation, PCCP 19 (2017) 20101-9.

Sustainable Energy & Fuels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Carolan, C. Rocks, D.B. Padmanaban, P. Maguire, V. Svrcek, D. Mariotti, Environmentally friendly nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for next generation solar cells, Sustainable Energy & Fuels 1 (2017) 1611-9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phenyl-doped graphitic carbon nitride: photoluminescence mechanism and latent fingerprint imaging
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Song, Z. Li, L. Lin, Y. Zhang, T. Lin, L. Chen, et al., Phenyl-doped graphitic carbon nitride: photoluminescence mechanism and latent fingerprint imaging, Nanoscale 9 (2017) 17737-42.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Probing energy and electron transfer mechanisms in fluorescence quenching of biomass carbon quantum dots
ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Liang, M. Kang, G.F. Payne, X. Wang, R. Sun, Probing energy and electron transfer mechanisms in fluorescence quenching of biomass carbon quantum dots, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 8 (2016) 17478-17488.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yao-Te Yen is an investigator in the Department of Forensic Science, Investigation Bureau, Ministry of Justice, Taiwan. He received his MS degree from the Department of Chemistry at National Tsing Hua University in 2007. He has been working for development of analytic methods in illicit drugs. Currently, he is a PhD candidate in prof. Huan-Tsung Chang’s group at National Taiwan University. He is interested in mass spectrometry, isotope ratio mass spectrometry, nanomaterials, and sensors.

Yu-Syuan Lin is pursuing his Ph.D. at the Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University in prof. Huan-Tsung Chang’s research group. He received his M.S. at the Department of Applied Science of National Taitung University, Taiwan in 2017. He is working on the development of fluorescence nanomaterial for analysis of environment sample.

Tzu-Heng Chen is a postdoctoral researcher in the prof. Huan-Tsung Chang’s group at National Taiwan University. He received his PhD degree from the Department of Chemistry at National Taiwan University in 2019. He is interested in optical properties and self-assembling of fluorescent nanomaterials and their application.

San-Chong Chyueh is currently a section chief in the Department of Forensic Science, Investigation Bureau, Ministry of Justice, Taiwan. He obtained his PhD degree from the Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University in 1983. He is interested in mass spectrometry and analytic instrument.

Huan-Tsung Chang is currently a Professor of the Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University. He obtained his PhD from the Department of Chemistry, Iowa State University, in 1994 with Dr Edward S. Yeung. His current research interests include nanotechnology, green chemistry, biosensors, and mass spectrometry.
